event: Polynesian voyagers settle Pitcairn and nearby Henderson Island, establishing small farming and fishing communities.
Polynesian inhabitants quarry volcanic stone at Down Rope and produce adzes traded across the surrounding islands.
The Polynesian population vanishes, likely after resource depletion and the collapse of trade links left the island uninhabitable.
Portuguese navigator Pedro Fernandes de Queirós sights nearby islands in the region during his Pacific expedition.
British ship HMS Swallow under Philip Carteret sights the uninhabited island, named after midshipman Robert Pitcairn who first spotted it.
The mutiny on HMS Bounty occurs, with Fletcher Christian seizing the ship from Captain William Bligh.
Nine Bounty mutineers, six Tahitian men, and twelve Tahitian women settle Pitcairn and burn the Bounty to avoid detection.
Violent conflict between the mutineers and Tahitian men erupts, killing Fletcher Christian and most of the European and Polynesian men.
John Adams becomes the sole surviving adult man, leading the remaining women and children as a devout Christian community.
American sealing ship Topaz under Mayhew Folger rediscovers the island and the descendants of the Bounty mutineers.
British warships HMS Briton and HMS Tagus confirm the settlement, but Britain takes no immediate action against the colony.
Patriarch John Adams dies, leaving the small community he had guided for nearly three decades.
The entire population is relocated to Tahiti, but disease and homesickness prompt the survivors to return to Pitcairn.
Pitcairn formally becomes a British colony, adopting a written constitution and electing a magistrate, one of the first to enfranchise women.
The overcrowded community of 193 people is resettled to Norfolk Island aboard the Morayshire.
Several families, missing their home, sail back from Norfolk Island to re-establish the Pitcairn settlement.
A further group of returning families brings Pitcairn's population back to a sustainable level.
Seventh-day Adventist missionary John Tay visits and converts the islanders, who formally adopt Adventism the following year.
The community is baptized into the Seventh-day Adventist Church, reshaping Pitcairn's religious and social life.
Administration of Pitcairn is placed under the British High Commissioner for the Western Pacific.
Henderson, Ducie, and Oeno islands are formally annexed and incorporated into the Pitcairn group.
Pitcairn's recorded population peaks at around 233 residents before later decades of emigration.
Administration of the islands is transferred to the British High Commissioner for the Western Pacific based in Fiji.
Oversight of Pitcairn moves to the British High Commissioner in Fiji and later to the Governor following changes in regional administration.
Archaeologist and explorer Thor Heyerdahl surveys Pitcairn, documenting Polynesian-era stone artifacts and petroglyphs.
Responsibility for Pitcairn passes to the British High Commissioner in New Zealand after the Western Pacific High Commission is dissolved.
Pitcairn issues its own postage stamps as a key source of revenue for the tiny dependency.
The 200-nautical-mile exclusive economic zone is established around the islands, expanding Pitcairn's marine jurisdiction.
A landmark trial convicts several Pitcairn men of historical sexual offences, exposing decades of abuse and straining the community.
A new constitution comes into force, formalizing human rights protections and the governance structure of the territory.
Britain creates the Pitcairn Islands Marine Reserve, one of the world's largest, protecting roughly 830,000 square kilometres of ocean.
Pitcairn legalizes same-sex marriage, despite having no openly gay residents at the time.
Pitcairn launches campaigns offering free land to attract new settlers and reverse its dwindling population.
The COVID-19 pandemic forces Pitcairn to suspend visitor arrivals, deepening the isolation of one of the world's most remote communities.
Pitcairn's population falls to roughly 35 to 40 residents, raising concerns about the long-term viability of the settlement.
